sentence: Subjective:

The patient is a 64-year-old male who presents with a 3-4 month history of decreased vision, particularly in dimly lit environments, and difficulty adjusting to straight lines, which he describes as looking wavy.
He reports that his symptoms have worsened over the past few months.
The patient mentions that he and his wife dine out frequently, and he had a recent enjoyable experience at an Italian restaurant.
He denies any recent trauma or injury to the eye.
The patient admits to smoking a pack a day, despite previous attempts to quit.
He has a history of high blood pressure and admits to not taking his medication as prescribed.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of high blood pressure, for which he has been prescribed medication.
Review of systems:
The patient reports no other symptoms.
Current medications:
I did not discuss any other medications with the patient.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
The anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes.
The cornea is clear in both eyes.
The peak hole cup to disc ratio is 0.2 in the right eye and 0.3 in the left eye.
The fundus examination of the right eye shows the retina to be attached, with no holes or tears noted and RPE changes in the fovea.
The posterior segment exam of the right eye shows drusen in the macula, retinal thickening, and the presence of subretinal fluid and exudate accumulation, and is positive for a hemorrhage.

The examination of the left eye shows the cornea to be clear, no mvi, peak hole cup to disc ratio of 0.3, RPE changes in the fovea, no hemorrhage or subretinal fluid noted, and the retina is attached appropriately.
Test Results:
The OCT exam shows drusen deposits under the RPE, subretinal fluid accumulation, and choroidal neovascular membrane.
The fluorescein angiography reveals a focal area of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has neovascular age-related macular degeneration (AMD) in the right eye, with advanced disease that is likely to lead to severe vision loss.
Problem list:
Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) - confirmed
Plan:
I prescribed Lucentis injections, to be administered once a month, to slow the rate of vision decline and potentially prevent further vision loss.
I instructed the patient to take his blood pressure medication as prescribed to address his history of high blood pressure.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient in a couple of minutes to administer the first Lucentis injection.
